eccentric.note: From 'off-center,' meaning someone whose behavior or personality deviates from the norm; 'quirky,' 'odd.'
eccentric.translation: The eccentric professor wore mismatched socks every day.

elicit.note: From 'to lure out,' meaning to draw out information, a reaction, or a response from someone.
elicit.translation: The journalist tried to elicit a confession from the suspect.

elect.note: From 'to pick out from many,' meaning to choose by vote or to select.
elect.translation: The citizens will elect a new president next year.

enfold.note: Meaning 'to wrap inside,' to enclose or embrace something warmly.
enfold.translation: She enfolded her child in a warm hug.

engage.note: From 'to place within a pledge,' meaning to involve, employ, or betroth.
engage.translation: He was engaged to his college sweetheart.

entangle.note: Meaning 'to cause to go into a tangled state,' to make involved in complexity or difficulty.
entangle.translation: The fishing line became entangled in the propeller.

enter.note: To go or come into a place, organization, or situation.
enter.translation: Please enter through the main door.

enwrap.note: Similar to 'enfold,' meaning 'to wrap inside,' to cover or surround completely.
enwrap.translation: The fog enwrapped the city in silence.

epidermis.note: Meaning 'skin on top of the dermis,' referring to the outermost layer of the skin.
epidermis.translation: The epidermis protects the body from external harm.

epiphenomenon.note: A philosophical/scientific term for 'a phenomenon that appears alongside the main phenomenon,' a secondary byproduct.
epiphenomenon.translation: Some argue that consciousness is an epiphenomenon of brain activity.

episode.note: Originally a scene inserted between acts of a play; now means an installment of a series, an incident, or a distinct event.
episode.translation: The series finale was the most dramatic episode yet.

euphony.note: Meaning 'pleasant sound,' referring to the harmonious quality of sounds or words.
euphony.translation: The poem was praised for its euphony and rhythm.

eulogy.note: Meaning 'good speech,' a tribute or speech of praise, especially for someone who has died.
eulogy.translation: He delivered a moving eulogy at his father's funeral.

euphemism.note: The use of a mild or indirect expression instead of a harsh one; a 'good word' substituted for an unpleasant one.
euphemism.translation: Passed away is a euphemism for died.

excrete.note: From 'to sift and send out,' meaning to eliminate waste products from the body.
excrete.translation: The kidneys excrete waste products through urine.

ex-president.note: Meaning 'former president,' referring to someone who previously held the office.
ex-president.translation: The ex-president gave a speech at the ceremony.

extinct.note: From 'to completely extinguish,' meaning no longer existing as a species, or (of a volcano) no longer active.
extinct.translation: The dodo bird has been extinct for centuries.

exoskeleton.note: Meaning 'skeleton on the outside of the body,' referring to the hard outer shell of insects and crustaceans.
exoskeleton.translation: Crabs have a hard exoskeleton that protects them.

exothermic.note: A scientific term meaning 'releasing heat outward' during a chemical reaction.
exothermic.translation: Combustion is an exothermic reaction that releases heat.

exterior.note: Meaning 'that which is further outside,' referring to the outside surface or part of something.
exterior.translation: The exterior of the building was painted white.

extracurricular.note: Meaning 'outside the regular curriculum,' referring to activities outside of formal academic coursework.
extracurricular.translation: She participated in many extracurricular activities after school.

extraterrestrial.note: Meaning 'from outside the Earth,' referring to life or matter from beyond our planet.
extraterrestrial.translation: The film depicted an encounter with extraterrestrial beings.

forecast.note: From 'to cast a prediction ahead,' meaning to predict weather or future events.
forecast.translation: The weather forecast called for rain tomorrow.

forehead.note: Meaning 'the front part of the head,' referring to the area above the eyes.
forehead.translation: She wiped the sweat from her forehead.

foreshadow.note: Meaning 'to cast a shadow before,' to hint at or signal something that will happen later.
foreshadow.translation: The stormy weather foreshadowed the conflict to come.

foreshorten.note: In art, to draw an object shorter than it actually is to create a sense of depth; also means to shorten in time or space.
foreshorten.translation: The artist foreshortened the figure's arm for perspective.

hemisphere.note: Meaning 'half of a sphere,' referring to half of the Earth or half of the brain.
hemisphere.translation: Australia is located in the Southern Hemisphere.

hemicycle.note: Meaning 'half of a circle,' referring to a semicircle or a semicircular structure.
hemicycle.translation: The parliament seats were arranged in a hemicycle.

hemiplegia.note: A medical term meaning 'paralysis of half the body,' referring to one-sided paralysis.
hemiplegia.translation: Hemiplegia often results from a stroke.

heptagonal.note: Meaning 'having seven angles,' describing a seven-sided polygon.
heptagonal.translation: The heptagonal tile created a unique pattern on the floor.

heptathlon.note: An athletic competition consisting of seven events.
heptathlon.translation: She won gold in the heptathlon at the Olympics.

hexadecimal.note: Based on the number 16, a numeral system used in computing.
hexadecimal.translation: Colors in web design are often written in hexadecimal notation.

hexagon.note: Meaning 'a figure with six angles,' a six-sided polygon.
hexagon.translation: Honeycomb cells are naturally hexagonal in shape.

hexameter.note: In poetry, a line consisting of six metrical feet.
hexameter.translation: Homer's epics are written in dactylic hexameter.

immaculate.note: Meaning 'without a single stain,' referring to something perfectly clean or free from flaw.
immaculate.translation: Her white dress remained immaculate throughout the event.

immediate.note: Meaning 'with nothing in between,' referring to something happening right away or directly.
immediate.translation: She gave an immediate response to his question.

immature.note: Meaning 'not yet fully grown or developed,' referring to immaturity in age or behavior.
immature.translation: His immature behavior annoyed his coworkers.

immobile.note: Meaning 'unable to move,' referring to something stationary or incapable of movement.
immobile.translation: The injured player lay immobile on the field.

impractical.note: Meaning 'not practical,' referring to something unrealistic or without usefulness.
impractical.translation: The plan was creative but impractical to implement.

introduction.note: From 'to lead inside,' meaning a formal presentation or the opening section of a text.
introduction.translation: The book's introduction outlined the author's main argument.

isobar.note: A meteorological term for a line connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure.
isobar.translation: The meteorologist traced isobars on the weather map.

isometric.note: Meaning 'of equal length,' used in exercise (isometric contractions) or technical drawing.
isometric.translation: Isometric exercises improve strength without joint movement.

isosceles.note: Describing a triangle with two sides of equal length.
isosceles.translation: An isosceles triangle has two equal angles.

isotope.note: Atoms of the same element that occupy 'the same place' in the periodic table but have different mass numbers.
isotope.translation: Carbon-14 is a radioactive isotope used in dating.

kilocalorie.note: Meaning '1000 calories,' the unit commonly used to measure food energy, often simply called a calorie.
kilocalorie.translation: A typical adult needs about 2000 kilocalories per day.

kilogram.note: Meaning '1000 grams,' the base unit of mass in the metric system.

kilometer.note: Meaning '1000 meters,' a unit of distance in the metric system.
kilometer.translation: The nearest town is ten kilometers away.

kilowatt.note: Meaning '1000 watts,' a unit of electrical power.
kilowatt.translation: The generator produces 5 kilowatts of power.

midday.note: Meaning 'the middle of the day,' referring to noon.
midday.translation: They stopped for lunch at midday.

midnight.note: Meaning 'the middle of the night,' referring to 12 o'clock at night.
midnight.translation: The party lasted until well past midnight.

midpoint.note: Meaning 'the exact middle point of something.'
midpoint.translation: The midpoint of the line segment was marked in red.

midsummer.note: Meaning 'the middle of summer,' referring to the peak of summer or the summer solstice.
midsummer.translation: The festival is held every year at midsummer.

millennium.note: Meaning 'a period of a thousand years.'
millennium.translation: The new millennium began on January 1, 2001.

milliliter.note: Meaning 'one-thousandth of a liter,' a unit of volume.
milliliter.translation: Add 250 milliliters of water to the mixture.

millimeter.note: Meaning 'one-thousandth of a meter,' a unit of length.
millimeter.translation: The gap was only a few millimeters wide.

millipede.note: Meaning 'having a thousand legs,' an arthropod with many legs.
millipede.translation: A millipede curled into a ball when threatened.

monochromatic.note: Meaning 'consisting of one color,' describing art or design using shades of a single color.

monogamy.note: The practice of having 'one marriage' or one partner at a time.
monogamy.translation: Monogamy is the most common form of marriage worldwide.

monogram.note: A design made of combined initials, where multiple letters appear as one.
monogram.translation: Her luggage was embossed with a gold monogram.

monolith.note: Meaning 'a single large stone,' referring to a large single stone or a large unified organization.
monolith.translation: Stonehenge is composed of enormous monoliths.

nanometer.note: From Greek 'nanos' (dwarf), meaning 'one billionth of a meter,' used in nanotechnology.
nanometer.translation: The transistor is only a few nanometers wide.

nanosecond.note: Meaning 'one billionth of a second,' an extremely short unit of time.
nanosecond.translation: Modern computers can perform operations in nanoseconds.

nanotechnology.note: Technology that operates at the nanometer scale, manipulating matter at the atomic level.
nanotechnology.translation: Nanotechnology is revolutionizing medicine and materials science.

noncompliant.note: Meaning 'not following rules or requirements,' describing someone or something that fails to comply.
noncompliant.translation: The factory was shut down for being noncompliant with safety regulations.

nonconformist.note: A person who does not follow conventional norms or practices.
nonconformist.translation: He was a nonconformist who refused to follow trends.

nonfiction.note: Writing that is based on real events and facts, not fictional stories.
nonfiction.translation: She preferred reading nonfiction to novels.

nonsense.note: Words or ideas that are meaningless or absurd.
nonsense.translation: He dismissed the conspiracy theory as complete nonsense.

oblong.note: Having a rectangular shape that is longer than it is wide.
oblong.translation: The room had an oblong table in the center.

obstruct.note: From 'to build up blocking the way,' meaning to block or impede.
obstruct.translation: Fallen trees obstructed the road after the storm.

obtrusive.note: Tending to push forward inappropriately; noticeable in an unwelcome way.
obtrusive.translation: The advertisement was too obtrusive to ignore.

octave.note: Meaning 'the eighth note,' an interval spanning eight notes in music.
octave.translation: She sang the melody an octave higher than written.

octagon.note: Meaning 'a figure with eight angles,' an eight-sided polygon.
octagon.translation: Stop signs are octagonal in shape.

octopus.note: Meaning 'an animal with eight legs,' a marine creature.
octopus.translation: The octopus changed color to camouflage itself.

October.note: Originally the eighth month in the Roman calendar; now the tenth month.
October.translation: The leaves turn red and orange in October.

omnipotence.note: Meaning 'the power to do all things,' referring to unlimited power.
omnipotence.translation: God's omnipotence is a central concept in many religions.

omnipresence.note: The quality of being present everywhere at the same time.
omnipresence.translation: The omnipresence of smartphones has changed daily life.

omniscience.note: Meaning 'the knowledge of all things,' referring to unlimited knowledge.
omniscience.translation: An omniscient narrator knows all characters' thoughts.

overestimate.note: To estimate something to be greater than it actually is.
overestimate.translation: Don't overestimate how much time you have.

overpay.note: To pay more than is required or reasonable.
overpay.translation: He felt he had overpaid for the car.

overwhelm.note: From 'to overturn by covering over,' meaning to be overcome by emotion or to defeat decisively.
overwhelm.translation: She was overwhelmed by the amount of work.

panacea.note: Meaning 'a remedy for all diseases,' a solution to all problems.
panacea.translation: There is no panacea for economic inequality.

pandemic.note: A disease that has spread to 'all people,' affecting a worldwide population.
pandemic.translation: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted lives globally.

pantheism.note: The belief that 'all is god,' identifying the divine with the universe.
pantheism.translation: Pantheism sees nature itself as divine.

pentad.note: A group or set of five, or a period of five years.
pentad.translation: The study tracked changes over a pentad of years.

pentagon.note: Meaning 'a five-angled figure,' also the name of the US Department of Defense building.
pentagon.translation: The Pentagon is the headquarters of the US military.

pentameter.note: In poetry, a line consisting of five metrical feet.
pentameter.translation: Shakespeare often wrote in iambic pentameter.

pentadactyl.note: A biological term meaning 'having five digits,' describing limbs with five fingers or toes.
pentadactyl.translation: Humans have pentadactyl hands and feet.

pentatonic.note: A musical scale consisting of five notes.
pentatonic.translation: Many folk melodies are based on a pentatonic scale.

posterior.note: Meaning 'further to the rear,' referring to the back part of something, or informally the buttocks.
posterior.translation: The posterior of the brain controls vision.

posthumous.note: Meaning 'after being buried (dying),' referring to something occurring after someone's death.

postmodern.note: Referring to artistic and intellectual movements that came after modernism.
postmodern.translation: The building was a bold example of postmodern architecture.

postpartum.note: A medical term meaning 'after childbirth,' relating to the period following delivery.
postpartum.translation: Postpartum depression affects many new mothers.

prepare.note: To make ready beforehand for some purpose.
prepare.translation: She stayed up late to prepare for the exam.

preview.note: Meaning 'a viewing before the main event,' such as a film trailer or advance screening.
preview.translation: The preview gave away too much of the plot.

pro-choice.note: Supporting an individual's right to choose, especially regarding abortion.
pro-choice.translation: She was a vocal pro-choice advocate.

pro-life.note: Opposing abortion and supporting the right to life of the fetus.
pro-life.translation: The pro-life group held a rally downtown.

protagonist.note: Meaning 'the first actor,' the main character in a story.
protagonist.translation: The protagonist faces many challenges throughout the novel.

pronoun.note: Meaning 'a word used in place of a noun,' a grammatical category including he, she, they.
pronoun.translation: She asked everyone to use their correct pronouns.

protoplasm.note: Meaning 'the first, basic substance,' the living matter within a cell.
protoplasm.translation: Protoplasm is the material basis of all living cells.

prototype.note: Meaning 'the first model,' an original example from which copies are made.
prototype.translation: Engineers tested the prototype before mass production.

protozoa.note: Meaning 'the first, most primitive animals,' single-celled organisms.
protozoa.translation: Protozoa are among the simplest forms of life.

pseudonym.note: Meaning 'a false name,' a pen name or alias used instead of one's real name.
pseudonym.translation: The author published the book under a pseudonym.

pseudopod.note: Meaning 'a false foot,' temporary projections used by amoebas for movement and feeding.
pseudopod.translation: The amoeba extended a pseudopod to engulf its prey.

pseudoscience.note: Claims that appear scientific but lack the basis of real scientific methodology.
pseudoscience.translation: Astrology is often classified as a pseudoscience.

quadruped.note: Meaning 'an animal with four feet,' a four-legged animal.
quadruped.translation: Dogs, cats, and horses are all quadrupeds.

quadrilateral.note: Meaning 'a figure with four sides,' any four-sided polygon.
quadrilateral.translation: A square is a special type of quadrilateral.

quadruplet.note: One of four children born at the same birth.
quadruplet.translation: The couple was surprised to learn they were expecting quadruplets.

quatrain.note: A stanza in poetry consisting of four lines.
quatrain.translation: The sonnet is composed of three quatrains and a couplet.

quart.note: A unit of volume equal to one quarter of a gallon.
quart.translation: The recipe called for a quart of milk.

quarter.note: One fourth of something; also 15 minutes, or a 25-cent coin.
quarter.translation: She paid with a quarter at the vending machine.

quartet.note: A group of four musicians or a piece of music for four performers.
quartet.translation: The string quartet performed Beethoven's opus 18.

quintet.note: A group of five musicians or a piece of music for five performers.
quintet.translation: The jazz quintet played all evening.

quintuplet.note: One of five children born at the same birth.

quintessence.note: In ancient philosophy, the fifth element; now used to mean the purest embodiment of something.
quintessence.translation: She is the quintessence of elegance.

quintessential.note: Representing the most perfect or typical example of something.
quintessential.translation: Pizza is considered the quintessential Italian food.

septet.note: A group of seven musicians or a piece of music for seven performers.
septet.translation: Beethoven wrote a famous septet for wind and string instruments.

septuagenarian.note: A person who is in their seventies.
septuagenarian.translation: The septuagenarian still ran marathons every year.

septuple.translation: The investment yielded a septuple return over ten years.

sexpartite.translation: The ceiling was decorated with a sexpartite vault.

sextet.note: A group of six musicians or a composition for six performers.
sextet.translation: The jazz sextet played beautifully at the festival.

sextant.note: Named for the one-sixth arc of a circle it uses; a navigational instrument for measuring the altitude of celestial bodies.
sextant.translation: Sailors once relied on a sextant to determine their position at sea.

sextuplet.note: One of six children born at the same birth.
sextuplet.translation: The birth of sextuplets made national headlines.

supramolecular.note: Relating to chemistry that operates 'above the single molecule level,' studying complex structures formed by intermolecular interactions.
supramolecular.translation: Supramolecular chemistry explores how molecules interact and assemble.

supraorbital.note: An anatomical term meaning 'above the eye socket (orbit).'
supraorbital.translation: The supraorbital ridge is the bony ridge above the eye socket.

supreme.translation: The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body in the country.

supremacy.translation: The nation sought naval supremacy over its rivals.

symbol.note: From Greek 'to throw together (and match),' a token that represents an abstract concept; a 'sign' or 'emblem.'
symbol.translation: The dove is a universal symbol of peace.

symphony.note: From Greek 'sounds sounding together,' meaning an 'orchestral composition' or 'harmony.'
symphony.translation: Beethoven's ninth symphony is a timeless masterpiece.

symmetry.note: From Greek 'measured together in proportion,' meaning 'balance' or 'correspondence of form.'
symmetry.translation: The human face has a natural symmetry.

tertiary.note: Meaning 'third in order,' referring to 'third-level' or 'tertiary' in rank or sequence.
tertiary.translation: Tertiary education refers to college or university level study.

tercet.note: A group of three lines forming a unit in poetry; a 'triplet.'
tercet.translation: The poem was composed entirely of tercets.

tertian.note: Occurring every third day (with one day between); used mainly to describe a type of malaria.
tertian.translation: Tertian malaria causes fever every other day.

terabyte.note: A unit of digital information equal to one trillion bytes, or approximately one thousand gigabytes.
terabyte.translation: The hard drive has a capacity of two terabytes.

teraflop.note: A measure of computing speed equal to one trillion floating-point operations per second.
teraflop.translation: Modern supercomputers can perform petaflops, far beyond teraflops.

terahertz.note: A unit of frequency equal to one trillion hertz, used in spectroscopy and imaging.
terahertz.translation: Terahertz imaging can see through clothing without harm.

tetrad.note: A group or set of four; a unit of four.
tetrad.translation: The researcher arranged the samples in tetrads.

tetrameter.note: In poetry, a line consisting of four metrical feet.
tetrameter.translation: Much of nursery rhyme poetry is written in tetrameter.

tetrahedron.note: A solid figure with four triangular faces; a 'four-faced' three-dimensional shape.
tetrahedron.translation: A tetrahedron is the simplest three-dimensional polyhedron.

Tetragrammaton.note: The four Hebrew letters (YHWH) representing the name of God in the Hebrew Bible.
Tetragrammaton.translation: Orthodox Jews avoid pronouncing the Tetragrammaton.

triangle.note: From Latin 'three-angled figure,' a polygon with three sides and three angles.
triangle.translation: She tapped the triangle in the orchestra pit.

triceratops.note: A dinosaur whose name means 'three-horned face,' featuring three horns on its head.
triceratops.translation: The triceratops used its horns to defend against predators.

tricuspid.note: Meaning 'having three cusps (pointed ends),' referring to the heart's tricuspid valve.
tricuspid.translation: The tricuspid valve controls blood flow between the right chambers of the heart.

triceps.note: Meaning 'muscle with three heads (origins),' the muscle at the back of the upper arm.
triceps.translation: Push-ups are effective for building triceps strength.

triptych.note: A work of art divided into three panels, often hinged; a 'three-panel painting' or altarpiece.
triptych.translation: The altarpiece was a magnificent medieval triptych.

trinity.note: Meaning 'three forming one,' referring to the Christian doctrine of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
trinity.translation: The Holy Trinity is a central doctrine of Christianity.

ultraliberal.note: Meaning 'beyond ordinary liberalism,' extremely progressive in political views.
ultraliberal.translation: Some critics labeled his policies as ultraliberal.

ultramarine.note: From 'beyond the sea,' referring to the prized pigment originally imported from Asia; a deep blue color.
ultramarine.translation: The artist applied rich ultramarine to the sky in the painting.

ultrasonic.note: Meaning 'beyond the range of sounds humans can hear,' relating to frequencies above 20 kHz.
ultrasonic.translation: Ultrasonic sensors are used in parking assistance systems.

ultraviolet.note: Meaning 'light beyond the violet end of the visible spectrum,' invisible to the human eye.
ultraviolet.translation: Ultraviolet rays from the sun can cause skin damage.

underage.note: Meaning 'below the legally defined age,' especially for activities like drinking or voting.
underage.translation: It is illegal to sell alcohol to underage customers.

underrated.note: Rated lower than actual merit; not appreciated enough.
underrated.translation: That film is criminally underrated by critics.

underrepresented.note: Not having sufficient representation relative to one's proportion in the population.
underrepresented.translation: Women remain underrepresented in senior leadership roles.

understated.note: Expressed in a deliberately restrained way; 'subtle,' 'understated.'
understated.translation: Her understated elegance made a lasting impression.

update.note: To bring to a current state; to 'refresh' or 'inform with new information.'
update.translation: Please update your software to the latest version.

uphold.note: To 'hold up from below,' meaning to support or maintain a law, principle, or decision.
uphold.translation: The judge upheld the lower court's ruling.

upscale.note: At the higher end of a scale or market; 'luxury,' 'high-end.'
upscale.translation: They moved to an upscale neighborhood after the promotion.

upstage.note: The part of the stage farthest from the audience; as a verb, to draw attention away from someone else.
upstage.translation: The new actor tried to upstage the lead performer.

upturn.note: A turn toward a higher direction; an 'improvement' or 'rise,' especially in an economy.
upturn.translation: Economists predicted an economic upturn by next year.

withhold.note: To 'hold back and not give,' meaning to refuse to give or disclose something.
withhold.translation: The company withheld crucial information from investors.

within.note: From Old English meaning 'inside together with'; meaning 'inside' or 'not beyond a limit.'
within.translation: Please respond within two business days.

without.note: The opposite of within; meaning 'lacking' or 'in the absence of.'
without.translation: She completed the project without any help.

withstand.note: To 'stand firm against' hardship or attack; meaning to 'endure' or 'resist.'
withstand.translation: The bridge was built to withstand earthquakes.

acrid.note: Describing an unpleasantly sharp, bitter smell or taste that stings the nose or tongue.
acrid.translation: The acrid smell of smoke filled the room.

acumen.note: From Latin 'sharp point,' now used metaphorically to mean 'mental sharpness,' 'shrewdness,' or 'keen insight.'
acumen.translation: Her business acumen helped grow the company tenfold.

acute.note: Meaning 'sharpened'; used to describe sharpness of mind, severity of a condition, or a sharp angle.
acute.translation: The patient was admitted with acute chest pain.

acetic acid.note: The acid related to vinegar (acet), the main component of vinegar; also called ethanoic acid.
acetic acid.translation: Acetic acid gives vinegar its characteristic sour taste.

acetylene.note: A highly flammable hydrocarbon gas used in welding, derived from acetic acid chemistry.
acetylene.translation: Acetylene torches are used for cutting and welding metal.

acrobat.note: From Greek 'one who walks at heights,' referring to a performer of gymnastic feats.
acrobat.translation: The acrobat flipped through the air to the audience's delight.

acropolis.note: Greek for 'the highest city,' the elevated fortified citadel at the center of ancient Greek cities.
acropolis.translation: The Parthenon sits atop the Acropolis in Athens.

aerobic.note: Relating to 'life (bio) in air (aero)'; describing organisms that need oxygen, or exercise that uses oxygen to burn fat.
aerobic.translation: She does 30 minutes of aerobic exercise every morning.

aerial.note: Meaning 'of the air (aer)'; used as an adjective meaning 'airborne' or 'of aviation,' and as a noun meaning 'antenna.'
aerial.translation: The aerial photograph showed the extent of the flooding.

aerodynamic.note: Relating to 'the movement of air (aero)'; describing how forces act on objects in motion through air, or a streamlined shape.
aerodynamic.translation: Sports cars are designed to be aerodynamic for better performance.

agony.note: From Greek 'contest or struggle'; evolved to describe extreme physical or mental suffering.
agony.translation: He was in agony after twisting his ankle.

antagonist.note: One who 'struggles against (ant)' the protagonist; the opposing character in a story.
antagonist.translation: The antagonist plots to destroy the hero throughout the story.

agriculture.note: The combination of 'land (agri)' and 'cultivation (culture),' meaning 'farming' or 'the science of cultivating the soil.'
agriculture.translation: Agriculture is the backbone of rural economies.

agronomy.note: From 'field (agro)' and 'management (nomy),' the science of soil management and crop production.
agronomy.translation: Advances in agronomy have greatly increased crop yields.

albino.note: From Portuguese, used by explorers to describe people with a deficiency of melanin pigment; 'albino' refers to such a person or animal.
albino.translation: The albino tiger had white fur and pink eyes.

albumin.note: A protein first discovered in egg white (albumen); found in blood, milk, and other biological fluids.
albumin.translation: Low albumin levels can indicate liver disease.

albatross.note: From Arabic via Portuguese, originally referring to a pelican; now a large white seabird. Figuratively, a 'burden' or source of guilt.
albatross.translation: The debt became an albatross around his neck.

alimentary.note: Meaning 'relating to nourishment (ali)'; used in 'alimentary canal,' the digestive tube from mouth to anus.
alimentary.translation: The alimentary canal processes all food we eat.

alimony.note: From Latin 'sustenance, support'; legal term for financial support paid to a former spouse after divorce.
alimony.translation: She was awarded alimony following the divorce settlement.

alloy.note: A mixture of two or more metals fused together; combining different metals for enhanced properties.
alloy.translation: Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin.

altitude.note: From Latin 'high (alt) state'; the height above sea level, or the elevation of a celestial object.
altitude.translation: The plane was cruising at an altitude of 35,000 feet.

alto.note: From Latin 'altus (high)'; in music, originally the highest male voice, now referring to the lower female vocal range.
alto.translation: She sang alto in the school choir.

alter ego.note: Latin for 'another (alter) self (ego)'; a second self or a very close friend.
alter ego.translation: His stage persona was his alter ego, wild and fearless.

alternative.note: From 'to alternate (alter)'; something that can be chosen instead; 'option,' 'substitute.'
alternative.translation: Is there an alternative route to avoid the traffic?

amateur.note: From Latin 'one who loves (am) something,' doing it for passion rather than money; a non-professional.
amateur.translation: He was an amateur photographer who won several competitions.

amoretto.note: Italian diminutive of 'amore (love)'; a cherub or cupid figure in art.
amoretto.translation: The ceiling was painted with amorettos holding garlands.

amour propre.note: French for 'love (amour) of oneself (propre)'; self-esteem or self-respect.
amour propre.translation: His amour propre was wounded by the public criticism.

ample.note: Meaning 'large and wide'; describing space or quantity that is 'plentiful' or 'more than enough.'
ample.translation: There is ample time to finish before the deadline.

amplitude.note: Meaning 'largeness, breadth'; in physics, the maximum displacement of a wave from its rest position.

android.note: From Greek 'resembling a human (andr)'; an artificial being made to look like a person.
android.translation: The science fiction novel featured androids indistinguishable from humans.

androgynous.note: Having both 'male (andro)' and 'female (gyn)' characteristics; gender-neutral in appearance.
androgynous.translation: The model had an androgynous look that defied categorization.

angiogram.note: A medical image or record (gram) of blood vessels (angio), produced by X-ray after injecting contrast dye.
angiogram.translation: The angiogram revealed a blockage in the coronary artery.

angioplasty.note: A surgical procedure to widen (plasty) narrowed or blocked blood vessels (angio).
angioplasty.translation: He underwent angioplasty to restore blood flow to his heart.

annual.note: From Latin 'of the year (annu)'; meaning 'yearly' or 'occurring once a year.'
annual.translation: The company holds an annual shareholders meeting every spring.

annuity.note: A fixed sum paid yearly; an insurance or investment product providing regular annual income.
annuity.translation: She purchased an annuity to supplement her retirement income.

centennial.note: From 'hundred (cent) years (enni)'; meaning 'occurring every hundred years' or 'a 100th anniversary.'
centennial.translation: The city celebrated its centennial with a grand parade.

anther.note: The pollen-bearing part of a flower's stamen; from Greek 'flower (anthos).' Called the pollen sac in botany.
anther.translation: Bees collect pollen from the anther of flowers.

acanthus.note: A plant with spiny (ac) flowers (anthos); its distinctive leaf pattern was widely used in ancient Greek Corinthian column capitals.
acanthus.translation: The Corinthian column was topped with acanthus leaf carvings.

anthesis.note: The botanical term for the period of flowering (anth) in a plant.
anthesis.translation: Anthesis in cherry trees typically occurs in early spring.

anthropology.note: The study (logy) of humankind (anthrop); the science of human origins, culture, and society.
anthropology.translation: Cultural anthropology examines human societies around the world.

anthropoid.note: Meaning 'resembling a human (anthrop)'; referring to great apes or human-like creatures.
anthropoid.translation: Chimpanzees and gorillas are anthropoid primates.

philanthropy.note: From 'love (phil) of humankind (anthrop)'; charitable acts aimed at promoting human welfare.
philanthropy.translation: His philanthropy funded hospitals and schools across the region.

antique.note: From Latin 'antiquus (old, ancient)'; a valuable old object; something from the distant past.
antique.translation: She found a beautiful antique vase at the flea market.

antiquated.note: Made old; so outdated as to be no longer useful; 'old-fashioned.'
antiquated.translation: The factory still used antiquated machinery from the 1950s.

antiquity.note: The state of being ancient; referring to ancient times, especially ancient Greece and Rome.
antiquity.translation: The museum's collection spans antiquity to the Renaissance.

aperture.note: From Latin 'opened result'; the opening in a camera lens (aperture), or a gap or hole in a wall or rock.
aperture.translation: Increasing the aperture lets more light into the camera.

aperitif.note: From Latin 'to open the appetite'; a drink consumed before a meal to stimulate the appetite.
aperitif.translation: They sipped aperitifs while waiting for the first course.

apogee.note: The point in an orbit farthest from the Earth; figuratively, the highest point of achievement.
apogee.translation: The satellite reached its apogee before beginning its descent.

apocalypse.note: From Greek 'to uncover'; originally meaning 'revelation,' now mainly referring to the end of the world or a catastrophe.
apocalypse.translation: The film depicted an apocalypse caused by a viral outbreak.

appendage.note: Something 'hanging off' a main body; a limb or organ attached to an organism, or an attachment to a larger thing.
appendage.translation: Crustaceans use their appendages for swimming and feeding.

appendix.note: Something 'appended (hung onto)'; supplementary material at the end of a book, or the small intestinal appendix in anatomy.
appendix.translation: Further data is provided in the appendix of the report.

aquarium.note: A place (arium) for water (aqua); a tank or facility for keeping aquatic life.
aquarium.translation: The children were fascinated by the sharks at the aquarium.

aqueous.note: From Latin 'of water (aqua)'; meaning 'watery,' 'water-based,' or 'soluble in water.'
aqueous.translation: The solution was aqueous, containing dissolved salts.

Arbor Day.note: From Latin 'arbor (tree)'; a day dedicated to planting trees.
Arbor Day.translation: Students planted oak saplings on Arbor Day.

arboreal.note: From Latin 'of trees (arbor)'; describing animals that live in trees.
arboreal.translation: Koalas are arboreal animals that spend most of their lives in eucalyptus trees.

arboretum.note: A place where trees (arbor) are collected and grown for study and display; a 'tree garden.'
arboretum.translation: The university arboretum has over 300 species of trees.

archaic.note: Of or from the earliest period (arch); no longer in common use; 'ancient,' 'outdated.'
archaic.translation: The archaic language of the text made it hard to read.

archeology.note: The study (logy) of ancient (archeo) things; the science of human history through excavation of artifacts.
archeology.translation: Archeology has revealed much about ancient Egyptian civilization.

archive.note: From Greek 'government building'; a collection of historical records or the place where they are kept.
archive.translation: The national archive holds documents dating back centuries.

artery.note: Ancient Greeks thought arteries carried air; now specifically the blood vessel that carries blood away from the heart.
artery.translation: The surgeon repaired the blocked artery during the operation.

arteriosclerosis.note: The hardening (sclero) of arteries (arterio); a disease in which artery walls thicken and lose elasticity.
arteriosclerosis.translation: Arteriosclerosis increases the risk of heart attack and stroke.

arthritis.note: Inflammation (itis) of the joints (arthr); a medical term for joint disease.
arthritis.translation: Her arthritis made it difficult to grip objects.

arthropod.note: An animal with jointed (arthro) legs (pod); the phylum including insects, spiders, and crustaceans.
arthropod.translation: Insects are the most diverse group of arthropods on Earth.

articulate.note: From Latin 'to divide into joints'; to express thoughts clearly, or to join by means of a joint.
articulate.translation: She was able to articulate her ideas clearly in the presentation.

articulation.note: The act of dividing into joints; clear pronunciation, or a joint in anatomy.
articulation.translation: Good articulation is essential for public speaking.

aster.note: Named for its star-shaped flower; a genus of flowering plants in the daisy family.
aster.translation: Purple asters bloomed in the garden in autumn.

asteroid.note: A celestial body that 'resembles a star (aster)' but is smaller than a planet; a rocky body orbiting the sun.
asteroid.translation: Scientists tracked the asteroid as it passed near Earth.

astronaut.note: From Greek 'star (astro) sailor (naut)'; a person trained to travel in space.
astronaut.translation: The astronaut spent six months aboard the International Space Station.

astronomy.note: The science of 'the laws (nomy) of the stars (astro)'; the study of celestial objects and the universe.
astronomy.translation: Astronomy has expanded our understanding of the cosmos.

avian.note: From Latin 'of birds (avi)'; an adjective meaning 'relating to birds.'
avian.translation: The avian flu outbreak affected millions of poultry.

aviary.note: A place (ary) for birds (avi); a large enclosure for keeping birds.
aviary.translation: The zoo's aviary housed over fifty species of tropical birds.

aviator.note: One who flies like a bird; a pilot of an aircraft.
aviator.translation: The aviator wore a leather jacket and goggles.

axis.note: The central line around which something rotates; in mathematics, the reference line of a coordinate system.
axis.translation: The Earth rotates on its axis once every 24 hours.

axle.note: The rod or spindle passing through the center of a wheel; a 'wheel-shaft.'
axle.translation: The mechanic replaced the broken rear axle.

barometer.note: An instrument that measures (meter) atmospheric pressure (baro); figuratively, an indicator of change.
barometer.translation: A falling barometer often signals approaching bad weather.

baritone.note: From Greek 'heavy (bari) tone,' the male vocal range between tenor and bass.
baritone.translation: The baritone sang the lead role in the opera.

base.note: The bottom foundation of something; figuratively, the basis of an argument or a military installation.
base.translation: The statue stands on a marble base.

basal.note: Relating to or forming the base; 'fundamental.' In biology, the minimum level needed to sustain life, as in basal metabolic rate.
basal.translation: The basal metabolic rate measures the energy used at rest.

basis.note: Originally 'a place to stand'; the most important foundation or principle on which something rests.
basis.translation: Trust is the basis of any strong relationship.

bass.note: The lowest male vocal range, or the lowest-pitched instruments such as bass guitar or double bass.
bass.translation: He plays bass guitar in a rock band.

bibliography.note: From 'writing (graphy) about books (biblio)'; a list of references at the end of a paper or book.
bibliography.translation: The thesis included a detailed bibliography of over 50 sources.

bibliophile.note: A person who 'loves (phile) books (biblio)'; a book lover or collector.
bibliophile.translation: She was a dedicated bibliophile whose home was full of rare editions.

biology.note: The study (logy) of life (bio); the science of living organisms.
biology.translation: Biology class covered cell structure and genetics.

biosphere.note: The region (sphere) where life (bio) exists; the layer of Earth where all living organisms are found.
biosphere.translation: Human pollution threatens the entire biosphere.

biopsy.note: From 'seeing (opsy) living (bio) tissue'; the procedure of removing tissue for medical examination.
biopsy.translation: The doctor ordered a biopsy to test the suspicious growth.

bolus.note: Originally 'a lump of earth'; in medicine, a rounded lump of chewed food ready to swallow, or a single dose of injected drug.
bolus.translation: The bolus of food moved down the esophagus.

parabola.note: In geometry, the curve formed when a plane cuts a cone parallel to its slope; a 'U-shaped' curve.
parabola.translation: A thrown ball follows a parabolic arc.

parable.note: A story placed 'beside (para)' a moral truth to illustrate it; a short allegorical story with a lesson.
parable.translation: The Parable of the Prodigal Son is one of Jesus's most famous teachings.

botany.note: Originally referring to pasture grass; evolved to mean 'plants' in general and the science studying them.
botany.translation: Botany explores plant biology, ecology, and classification.

botanical.note: Relating to plants (botan); 'of botany' or 'derived from plants.'
botanical.translation: The botanical garden displayed thousands of plant species.

botanist.note: A person (ist) who studies plants (botan); a plant scientist.
botanist.translation: The botanist discovered three new species in the rainforest.

brachia.note: Plural of Latin 'bracchium (arm)'; used in anatomy to refer to arms or arm-like structures.
brachia.translation: The brachia of the starfish extended outward.

embrace.note: From 'pull into the arms (brace)'; to hug, or figuratively to accept or welcome an idea.
embrace.translation: She embraced her friend after years apart.

abbreviate.note: To make short (brevi); to shorten a word or phrase.
abbreviate.translation: The editor asked her to abbreviate the introduction.

brief.note: Short in time or length; also a legal summary document.
brief.translation: Please give a brief summary of the meeting.

brevity.note: The state of being short (brev); shortness of time or conciseness of expression.
brevity.translation: Brevity is the soul of wit.

bronchia.note: Plural of bronchus; the air passages branching from the trachea into the lungs.
bronchia.translation: Mucus builds up in the bronchia during a respiratory infection.

bronchitis.note: Inflammation (itis) of the bronchi (bronch); a respiratory disease.
bronchitis.translation: He was diagnosed with bronchitis after weeks of coughing.

bullet.note: From 'small round ball'; a projectile fired from a gun.
bullet.translation: The bullet was found lodged in the wall.

bulletin.note: From official sealed papal documents; now a short public announcement or news brief.
bulletin.translation: The school sent a bulletin to all parents about the event.

bursitis.note: Inflammation (itis) of a bursa (fluid-filled sac near a joint); causes joint pain.
bursitis.translation: He developed bursitis in his shoulder from repetitive motion.

disburse.note: To send money out from a purse (burse); to pay out or distribute funds.
disburse.translation: The grant money was disbursed to research institutions.

reimburse.note: To put money back (re) into the purse (burse); to repay someone for expenses.
reimburse.translation: The company will reimburse your travel expenses.

calcium.note: Named from lime (calc); a metallic element essential for bones and teeth.
calcium.translation: Milk is a rich source of calcium.

calcify.note: To make (ify) like limestone (calc); to harden through calcium salt deposits.
calcify.translation: The arteries began to calcify with age.

calculate.note: From 'use small pebbles (calculus)' as in Roman abacus counting; to compute or reckon.
calculate.translation: She calculated the total cost of the project.

calorie.note: A unit measuring heat (calor); the amount of energy in food.

caloric.note: Of or relating to heat (calor) or calories.
caloric.translation: The caloric content of the meal exceeded 800 calories.

calorimeter.note: A device that measures (meter) heat (calori); used in physics and chemistry experiments.
calorimeter.translation: The calorimeter measured the heat released by the reaction.

candle.note: From 'something that shines (cand)'; a wax stick burned to provide light.
candle.translation: She lit a candle when the power went out.

candent.note: Glowing with heat; emitting white light due to extreme heat. A rare literary word.
candent.translation: The candent metal glowed orange in the forge.

incandescent.note: Beginning to shine from within; 'glowing with heat,' brilliantly bright.
incandescent.translation: The incandescent bulb was eventually replaced by LED lights.

capillary.note: Hair-thin (capill) blood vessels; the smallest blood vessels connecting arteries and veins.
capillary.translation: Oxygen passes from capillaries into surrounding tissue.

capillarity.note: The phenomenon of liquid rising in a hair-thin tube against gravity; capillary action.
capillarity.translation: Plants use capillarity to draw water up from the roots.

capital.note: From 'head (capit)'; the most important city, financial assets, an uppercase letter, or of chief importance.
capital.translation: Paris is the capital of France.

decapitate.note: To cut off (de) the head (capit); to behead.
decapitate.translation: The statue was decapitated during the riot.

captain.note: The head (captain) of a group; the commander of a ship, team leader, or military rank.
captain.translation: The captain steered the ship through the storm.

carcinogen.note: A substance that generates (gen) cancer (carcino); any cancer-causing agent.
carcinogen.translation: Tobacco smoke contains dozens of carcinogens.

carcinoma.note: A malignant tumor (oma) arising from epithelial cells (carcin); a type of cancer.
carcinoma.translation: The biopsy confirmed it was a carcinoma.

cardiogram.note: A recording (gram) of heart (cardio) activity; an electrocardiogram (ECG).
cardiogram.translation: The cardiogram showed an irregular heartbeat.

cardiology.note: The study (logy) of the heart (cardio); the branch of medicine dealing with heart disease.
cardiology.translation: He specialized in cardiology after medical school.

carnage.note: From 'a mass of flesh'; widespread slaughter or great destruction.
carnage.translation: The battlefield was a scene of terrible carnage.

carnal.note: Of or relating to the flesh (carn); physical rather than spiritual, especially relating to sexual desires.
carnal.translation: The novel explored carnal and spiritual themes side by side.

carnival.note: From 'removing meat (carn)' before Lent; a festive celebration with rides, games, and entertainment.
carnival.translation: The children were excited to go to the carnival.

carnivore.note: An animal that eats (vore) meat (carni); a flesh-eating creature.
carnivore.translation: Lions are carnivores that hunt large prey.

carpus.note: The anatomical term for the group of bones forming the wrist.
carpus.translation: A fracture in the carpus can affect hand movement.

carpal-tunnel syndrome.note: A condition where the nerve in the wrist (carpal) tunnel is compressed, causing pain and numbness.
carpal-tunnel syndrome.translation: She developed carpal-tunnel syndrome from typing for hours each day.

eukaryote.note: An organism with a true (eu) nucleus (karyo); includes animals, plants, and fungi.
eukaryote.translation: Humans are eukaryotes because our cells have a defined nucleus.

prokaryote.note: An organism whose cells lack a nucleus enclosed by a membrane; bacteria are prokaryotes.
prokaryote.translation: Bacteria are prokaryotes that lack a true nucleus.

catheter.note: A thin tube sent down into the body to drain fluid or deliver medication.
catheter.translation: A catheter was inserted to drain the patient's bladder.

catharsis.note: The process of releasing and purging repressed emotions; emotional purification.
catharsis.translation: Watching the tragedy provided the audience with catharsis.

caustic.note: Capable of burning (caust) or corroding chemically; figuratively, bitingly sarcastic.
caustic.translation: Caustic soda can burn the skin on contact.

cauterize.note: To burn (cauter) a wound with heat or chemicals to stop bleeding or prevent infection.
cauterize.translation: The surgeon cauterized the wound to seal the blood vessels.

holocaust.note: From 'wholly (holo) burned (caust)'; originally a burnt offering; now refers to mass destruction, especially the Nazi genocide.
holocaust.translation: The Holocaust claimed the lives of six million Jews.

cave.note: A hollow (cav) space in rock or earth; a naturally formed underground chamber.
cave.translation: The explorers discovered cave paintings thousands of years old.

cavity.note: A hollow (cav) space; a hole in a tooth or an empty space in the body.
cavity.translation: The dentist found two cavities during the checkup.

concave.note: Hollowed inward (con + cav); curving inward like the inside of a bowl.
concave.translation: A concave mirror curves inward and focuses light.

cavalry.note: From mounted knights; troops on horseback; a military force of horse-mounted soldiers.
cavalry.translation: The cavalry charged across the open field.

cavalier.note: Originally a mounted knight; later came to mean showing a lack of proper concern, 'dismissive.'
cavalier.translation: His cavalier attitude toward safety alarmed the team.

chivalry.note: The code of conduct of medieval knights; courtesy and honorable behavior, especially toward women.
chivalry.translation: He held the door open — some say chivalry is not dead.

cell.note: From a monk's small room; now the basic structural unit of all living organisms, or a small enclosed space.
cell.translation: Every living organism is made of one or more cells.

cellular.note: Made of or relating to cells; in telecommunications, using a network of small coverage zones (cells).
cellular.translation: Cellular phones became widespread in the 1990s.

census.note: From Roman registration of citizens' property; an official count of a population.
census.translation: The government conducts a census every ten years.

censor.note: From Roman officials who judged public morals; one who examines material and suppresses objectionable content.
censor.translation: The censor removed several scenes from the film.

consensus.note: From 'feeling or thinking together (con)'; general agreement among a group.
consensus.translation: The committee reached a consensus after hours of debate.

century.note: From a Roman unit of 100 soldiers; a period of 100 years.
century.translation: The 20th century saw extraordinary technological progress.

centimeter.note: One hundredth (centi) of a meter; a unit of length.
centimeter.translation: The ruler measured in centimeters and millimeters.

percent.note: Per (per) hundred (cent); a ratio expressed out of 100.
percent.translation: Only 30 percent of applicants were accepted.

centrifugal.note: Tending to flee (fugal) from the center (centri); the apparent outward force on rotating objects.
centrifugal.translation: Centrifugal force pushes passengers outward on a spinning ride.

centrifuge.note: A machine using centrifugal force to separate substances of different densities.
centrifuge.translation: The lab used a centrifuge to separate blood components.

centripetal.note: Directed toward (petal) the center (centri); the inward force keeping objects in circular motion.
centripetal.translation: Gravity provides the centripetal force for Earth's orbit.

ethnocentric.note: Placing one's own ethnic group (ethno) at the center (centric) of the world; judging others by one's own cultural standards.
ethnocentric.translation: An ethnocentric worldview can lead to misunderstanding other cultures.

cephalic.note: Relating to the head (cephal); an anatomical term meaning 'of or toward the head.'
cephalic.translation: The cephalic index measures the ratio of skull width to length.

cephalopod.note: An animal with feet (pod) on its head (cephalo); includes squid, octopus, and cuttlefish.

hydrocephalus.note: Abnormal accumulation of water (hydro) in the head (cephalus); a medical condition causing brain swelling.
hydrocephalus.translation: Hydrocephalus is treated by inserting a shunt to drain excess fluid.

biceps.note: A muscle with two (bi) heads (ceps) of origin; the large muscle on the front of the upper arm.
biceps.translation: He flexed his biceps to show off his strength.

forceps.note: From 'grasping hot (for) things (ceps)'; a hinged surgical instrument used to grip or extract.
forceps.translation: The surgeon used forceps to remove the foreign object.

concept.note: Something grasped (cept) together (con) in the mind; an abstract idea or mental image.
concept.translation: The concept of democracy has evolved over centuries.

interception.note: The act of seizing (cept) something in between (inter); catching something before it reaches its destination.
interception.translation: The cornerback made a spectacular interception.

perception.note: The act of thoroughly (per) taking in (cept) information through the senses; awareness or understanding.
perception.translation: Perception of color varies among individuals.

reception.note: The act of receiving (cept); a welcoming event, a hotel front desk, or signal reception.
reception.translation: The wedding reception was held in the garden.

cerebellum.note: The small (ellum) brain (cereb); the part of the brain at the back that controls movement and balance.
cerebellum.translation: Damage to the cerebellum can cause loss of coordination.

cerebral.note: Of or relating to the brain (cerebr); intellectual rather than emotional.
cerebral.translation: Chess is a highly cerebral game.

cerebrum.note: The Latin word for brain; the largest part of the brain responsible for thinking and language.
cerebrum.translation: The cerebrum is divided into left and right hemispheres.

cervical.note: Relating to the neck (cervic); used for the neck vertebrae or the cervix of the uterus.
cervical.translation: She had a cervical spine injury from the accident.

cervix.note: Latin for neck; in anatomy, the neck-like lower part of the uterus.
cervix.translation: Regular Pap smears check for cervical cancer.

chemical.note: From alchemy; relating to chemistry or a substance produced by a chemical process.
chemical.translation: The factory was fined for releasing chemical waste.

chemotherapy.note: Treatment (therapy) using chemical (chemo) drugs; primarily used to treat cancer.
chemotherapy.translation: She underwent six months of chemotherapy.

chlorine.note: Named for its pale green (chlor) color; a toxic gas used in disinfection and manufacturing.
chlorine.translation: Chlorine is added to swimming pools to kill bacteria.

chlorophyll.note: The substance that makes leaves (phyll) green (chloro); essential for photosynthesis.
chlorophyll.translation: Chlorophyll absorbs sunlight to power photosynthesis.

chloroplast.note: Green (chloro) particles (plast) in plant cells; the organelle where photosynthesis occurs.
chloroplast.translation: Chloroplasts give plant leaves their green color.

cholera.note: Once thought caused by excess bile (chol); now known as a bacterial waterborne disease causing severe diarrhea.
cholera.translation: Cholera outbreaks often follow natural disasters.

cholesterol.note: A solid (stero) alcohol first found in bile (chole); a lipid essential for cell membranes.
cholesterol.translation: High cholesterol increases the risk of heart disease.

hypochondriac.note: From the belief that illness originated below (hypo) the cartilage (chondr) of the ribs; now someone excessively worried about their health.
hypochondriac.translation: She was a hypochondriac who went to the doctor for every minor symptom.

mitochondria.note: Named because they look like threads (mito) or granules (chondr) under a microscope; the cell's energy-producing organelles.
mitochondria.translation: Mitochondria are often called the powerhouses of the cell.

chromatic.note: Of or relating to color (chromat); in music, using all semitones including those outside the main scale.
chromatic.translation: The chromatic scale includes all twelve notes in an octave.

chromosome.note: Named because they take up colored (chromo) stain under a microscope; structures in cells that carry genetic information.
chromosome.translation: Humans have 46 chromosomes in each cell.

chromoplast.note: Colored (chromo) particles (plast) in plant cells; responsible for the pigments in flowers and fruits.
chromoplast.translation: Chromoplasts give carrots their orange color.

cinema.note: From 'moving (kine) pictures'; a movie or a movie theater.
cinema.translation: They went to the cinema to see the new film.

cinematic.note: Of or relating to cinema; having the qualities of a film.
cinematic.translation: The director had a distinctive cinematic style.

cinematography.note: The art of recording (graphy) motion (cinemato); the craft of film photography.
cinematography.translation: The film won an Oscar for its stunning cinematography.

excise.note: To cut out (ex + cise); to remove text by editing, or to surgically remove tissue.
excise.translation: The editor excised two unnecessary paragraphs.

incise.note: To cut into (in + cise) a surface; to engrave or make a surgical incision.
incise.translation: The surgeon incised the skin to reach the tumor.

incisor.note: A tooth (incisor) that cuts (incise); the front teeth used for biting food.
incisor.translation: Rabbits have large incisors for gnawing on vegetation.

scissors.note: From Latin for cutting (cis) tool; always plural because two blades work together.
scissors.translation: She used scissors to cut the wrapping paper.

class.note: From Roman social divisions based on wealth; now means a lesson, a social stratum, a category, or a grade.
class.translation: The biology class met every Tuesday morning.

class-action.note: A lawsuit (action) filed collectively by a group (class) of people with a common grievance.
class-action.translation: The consumers filed a class-action lawsuit against the company.

classification.note: The process of arranging things into classes or categories; systematic grouping.
classification.translation: The Linnaean classification system organizes all living things.

clastic.note: In geology, describing rock composed of broken (clast) fragments of other rocks.
clastic.translation: Sandstone is a clastic sedimentary rock.

iconoclastic.note: From destroying (clast) religious icons (icono); now means challenging traditional beliefs or institutions.
iconoclastic.translation: His iconoclastic ideas challenged decades of scientific orthodoxy.

pyroclastic.note: Relating to fragments (clast) of burning (pyro) rock expelled by a volcano.
pyroclastic.translation: The pyroclastic flow destroyed everything in its path.

closet.note: A small (et) closed (clos) space; a built-in storage space for clothes.
closet.translation: She organized her closet by color and season.

climate.note: From Greek for 'slope (klima)' of the Earth; the long-term weather pattern of a region.
climate.translation: The climate in the tropics is warm and humid year-round.

climatic.note: Of or relating to climate; describing weather patterns.
climatic.translation: The region experienced severe climatic changes over the decade.

climax.note: From ascending a slope (clima) to the highest point; the most intense or exciting point of a story or event.
climax.translation: The climax of the novel leaves the reader breathless.
